perturbation.original_text: Payment to the following individual below within 7 days upon completion and handover on the Project to VAL.
perturbation.changed_text: Payment to the following individual below within 60 days upon completion and handover on the Project to VAL.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment term to 60 days after completion may violate wage payment laws in jurisdictions like California, which require more prompt payment.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 201 requires that when an employer discharges an employee, the wages earned and unpaid at the time of discharge are due and payable immediately. While this contract is with developers, misclassifying employees as contractors is a common issue.  A 60-day payment window clearly violates the immediate payment requirement upon termination (discharge).

Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 201. (a) If an employer discharges an employee, the wages earned and unpaid at the time of discharge are due and payable immediately. An employer who lays off a group of employees by reason of the termination of seasonal employment in the curing, canning, or drying of any variety of perishable fruit, fish, or vegetables, shall be deemed to have made immediate payment when the wages of said employees are paid within a reasonable time as necessary for computation and payment thereof; provided, however, that the reasonable time shall not exceed 72 hours, and further provided that payment shall be made by mail to any employee who so requests and designates a mailing address therefor. (b) Notwithstanding any other law, the state employer shall be deemed to have made an immediate payment of wages under this section for any unused or accumulated vacation, annual leave, holiday leave, or time off to which the emp
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Paydays, pay periods, and the final wages Labor Commissioner's Office Paydays, pay periods, and the final wages In California, wages, with some exceptions (see table below), must be paid at least twice during each calendar month on the days designated in advance as regular paydays. The employer must establish a regular payday and is required to post a notice that shows the day, time and location of payment. Labor Code Section 207 Wages earned between the 1 st and 15 th days, inclusive, of any calendar month must be paid no later than the 26 th day of the month during which the labor was performed, and wages earned between the 16 th and last day of the month must be paid by the 10 th day of the following month. Other payroll periods such as weekly, biweekly (every two weeks) or semimonthly (twice per month) when the earning period is something other than between the 1 st and 15 th , and 16 th and last day of the month, must be paid within seven calendar days of the end of the payroll period within which the wages were earned. Labor Code Section 204 Overtime wages must be paid no later than the payday for the next regular payroll period following the payroll period in which the overtime wages were earned. An employer shall be in compliance with Labor Code Section 226(a) relating to total hours worked by the employee if the overtime hours are recorded as a correction on the itemized statement for the next regular pay period and include the dates of the pay period for which the correction is being made. Labor Code Section 204(b)(2) An employee who is discharged must be paid all of his or her wages, including accrued vacation, immediately at the time of termination. Labor Code Sections 201 and 227.3 A group of employees who are laid off by reason of the termination of seasonal employment in the curing, canning, or drying of any variety of perishable fruit, fish or vegetables, must be paid within 72 hours after the layoff. Payment shall be made by mail to any such employee who so requests and designates a mailing address therefor. Labor Code Section 201 An employee engaged in the production of motion pictures who is laid off and whose unusual or uncertain terms of employment require special computation in order to ascertain the amount due, must be paid by the next regular payday. The payment of wages to employees covered by this section may be mailed to the employee or made available to the employee at a location specified by the employer in the county where the employee was hired or performed labor. The payment shall be deemed to have been made on the date that the employee's wages are mailed to the employee or made available to the employee at the location specified by the employer, whichever is earlier. For purposes of this section, an employment terminates when the employment relationship ends, whether by discharge, lay off, resignation, completion of employment for a specified term, or otherwise. Labor Code Section 201.5 An employee engaged in the business of oil drilling who is laid off must be paid within 24 hours after discharge, excluding Saturdays, Sundays, and holidays. Labor Code Section 201.7 If employees are employed at a venue that hosts live theatrical or concert events and are enrolled in and routinely dispatched to employment through a hiring hall or other system of regular short-term employment established in accordance with a bona fide collective bargaining agreement, these employees and their employers may establish terms in their collective bargaining agreement the time limits for payment of wages to an employee who is discharged or laid off. Labor Code Section 201.9 An employee without a written employment contract for a definit
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment term to 60 days, contradicting California Labor Code Section 201, which mandates immediate payment of wages upon discharge. Snippet 2 supports this, stating, 'An employee who is discharged must be paid all of his or her wages, including accrued vacation, immediately at the time of termination.' While the contract might not explicitly cover discharged employees (as it's for developers), the law explanation raises concerns about employee misclassification, making the 60-day payment term a potential violation. The original text was much closer to legally allowed time, but the new 60 days constitutes a blatant violation.

perturbation.original_text: VAL shall, in the event of Developer committing any breach of any of the terms and conditions of this agreement or for any other reason considered as sufficient, be entitled to terminate this agreement by giving two (2) weeks' notice in writing and it is applicable only when the project is not completed.
perturbation.changed_text: VAL shall, in the event of Developer committing any breach of any of the terms and conditions of this agreement or for any other reason considered as sufficient, be entitled to terminate this agreement by giving one (1) day's notice in writing and it is applicable only when the project is not completed.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination to one day may be considered an unreasonable and unlawful restraint of trade, especially considering the developer's investment of time and resources. Some jurisdictions require longer notice periods or justification for termination to protect independent contractors.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Hong Kong law doesn't have a specific statute dictating notice periods, Section 7 of the Contracts Ordinance relates to the construction and interpretation of contracts, stating that terms must be reasonable. A one-day notice for termination for potentially any 'sufficient reason' is likely to be deemed unreasonable by a Hong Kong court and an unfair labor practice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Hong Kong Legal Information Institute (HKLII) We're sorry but Hong Kong Legal Information Institute (HKLII) doesn't work properly without JavaScript enabled. Please enable it to continue.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified a two-week notice for termination, while the changed text reduces this to one day. The Law Explanation suggests that a one-day notice for termination for 'any sufficient reason' may be deemed unreasonable under Hong Kong law, specifically referencing Section 7 of the Contracts Ordinance regarding the reasonableness of contract terms. While the scraped snippets provide limited direct support due to their incompleteness (Snippet 1 is empty, Snippet 2 is a JS error message), the Perturbation Explanation highlights the potential for this reduction to be considered an unreasonable and unlawful restraint of trade, especially considering the developer's investment. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition, where the time-sensitive obligation (notice period) does not align with potentially legal requirements for reasonableness, making the terms unenforceable. The change directly undermines the implied legal obligation of fair notice, thus a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Change Orders do not however cover any bug or glitch fixing produced out of the code written by Developer as any "bug" will be fixed by Developer for free up to 3 months after final delivery (Bug Fixing Warranty).
perturbation.changed_text: Change Orders do not however cover any bug or glitch fixing produced out of the code written by Developer as any "bug" will be fixed by Developer for free up to 2 weeks after final delivery (Bug Fixing Warranty).
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the bug fixing warranty to 2 weeks may violate consumer protection laws if the app malfunctions shortly after that period, impacting users in Hong Kong, especially if it doesn't meet a reasonable standard of quality and usability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Sale of Goods Ordinance (Chapter 26) Section 16 implies conditions as to quality or fitness. If the app is not of reasonable quality after the 2 weeks warranty, it could be said to violate the implied quality as determined under the ordinance, particularly relevant as the app will be sold to customers.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Hong Kong Legal Information Institute (HKLII) We're sorry but Hong Kong Legal Information Institute (HKLII) doesn't work properly without JavaScript enabled. Please enable it to continue.
perturbation.justification: The change from a 3-month bug fixing warranty to a 2-week warranty creates an inconsistency that may violate consumer protection laws. The Law Explanation mentions the Sale of Goods Ordinance (Chapter 26) Section 16, implying conditions as to quality or fitness, which suggests that a 2-week warranty might not be considered a reasonable period for an app to be of reasonable quality. The Perturbation Explanation highlights the potential violation if the app malfunctions shortly after the 2-week period. Although the scraped snippets are not helpful in this case, the shortened warranty period directly impacts the implied quality standards under the ordinance if the product fails shortly after. Thus, the modified term violates the federal or state law.
